Which of the following explains why the participants made errors in their recollections?

The participants had difficulty repeating the story because the details didn’t fit with their cultural schema.

q:
Anonymity in research-Clive Wearing

Since that time, Clive has not been able to encode new memories and, by some estimations, can only hold a thought for 7 to 9 seconds. Which of the following is an ethical consideration regarding the case of Clive Wearing?
o: Neither Clive nor his wife can provide their informed consent to be studied because of his condition.
The level of risk in studying Clive’s condition is too great.
We should not know Clive’s identity, especially while he is still living.
This information should only be published or publicly discussed after Clive’s death.

q:

She shows them a long series of numbers on a display for several seconds, and then has them write down as many numbers as they can remember in order. Dr. Adedeji has the students complete a survey in which she asks them “On a scale of 1 to 10, with 10 being highly effective, how well did you remember the numbers?” She concludes that her students have exceptionally good memories, as most of her students rated themselves as “highly effective.” Which of the following best explains Dr. Adedeji’s inaccurate conclusion?
o: Dr. Adedeji is exhibiting confirmation bias.
Dr. Adedeji designed her study with qualitative data.
Dr. Adedeji designed her study with quantitative data.
Dr. Adedeji’s survey led to self-report bias.

A type of memory that relates to accumulated knowledge about the world.

q:

Institutions are required to report data on intelligence test scores.
Research on intelligence often involves deception.

q:

In terms of ethical procedures, after the data are collected, it will be important for Dr. Adeyemi to do which of the following?
o: Fully debrief the participants on the deception involved in the study.
Report each participant’s results to the IRB.

Sources for Review: 1 2 3 Source 1 Introduction In this study, researchers examined whether taking notes on a laptop led to better memory recall than taking handwritten notes. Participants Sixty-seven students (33 men, 33 women, 1 not reported) were recruited from a participant pool at a university in New Jersey. Method Researchers chose five prerecorded lectures that lasted approximately 15 minutes each. Although these lectures did not cover common knowledge, the researchers chose topics that would be interesting to participants. In general, two participants viewed the lecture videos together in the same room, though some participants completed the study alone. The study room was preset with either laptops for computer-based notetaking or paper notebooks for handwritten notetaking, according to condition. The laptops had full-size keyboards (11 in × 4 in) and were not connected to the Internet. The lectures were projected onto a screen at the front of the room. Participants were instructed to use their preferred note-taking strategy of either writing notes by hand or taking notes using a laptop. The experimenter left the room while the lectures played. Next, participants were taken to a lab to complete a distractor task1and a challenging memory task. For the distractor task, using a laptop, participants were shown several arithmetic equations and asked to indicate for each one whether the answer was correct. For the memory task, they were given several letters to remember for later recall. After several presentations of unrelated material, a grid with letters was presented. Participants were asked to click on the letters they saw earlier during the task in the correct order. By the end of these tasks, approximately 30 minutes had elapsed since the end of the lecture participants had seen. Participants were asked both factual-recall questions (e.g., “Approximately how many years ago did the Indus civilization exist?”) and conceptual-application questions (e.g., “How do Japan and Sweden differ in their approaches to equality within their societies?”) related to the topics covered in the lectures; participants also completed several demographic measures. The responses were scored by raters unaware of the conditions. Ratings were consistent, but any disputes between the raters were resolved by discussion. Results and Discussion Participants performed equally well across conditions for factual-recall questions. However, on conceptual-application questions, participants who took notes using a laptop performed significantly worse than participants who took handwritten notes (p = 0.03)2. For each participant, the researchers determined the number of words in their lecture notes and the degree to which three-word chunks of text from a transcript of the lecture were present in those notes to determine the extent to which the notes from the lecture were verbatim.3Researchers found that notes taken using a laptop had a 14.6% verbatim overlap with the lecture, while notes taken by hand averaged an 8.8% verbatim overlap. Participants whose handwritten notes overlapped less with the verbatim lecture performed better on factual-recall and conceptual-application questions. Participants An earlier study, which was conducted in 2017 and included over 21,000 people, examined the effects of taking a multivitamin on health outcomes. From that study’s sample, over 7,000 people received a mailed invitation to participate in this study. Of those who received the invitation, almost 4,000 participants agreed to participate and were accepted. To be accepted, participants had to be over 65 years of age if women and over 60 years of age if men. In addition, they could not participate if they had ever had a stroke, if they had received a cancer diagnosis in the two years before the study, or if they had a history of any other serious illnesses. Participants had to be able to communicate in English and had to have access to an Internet-connected computer. A computer randomly assigned participants to two groups. Participants in Group 1 received a pack of multivitamins each month by mail and were instructed to take one pill twice a day. Participants in Group 2 received a pack of placebo pills in the same type of packaging as Group 1 and with the same instructions. The sample size of Group 1 was 1,758 people, and the sample size of Group 2 was 1,804 people. The demographics of each group are listed in the table: Demographics of Group 1 and Group 2 Demographic Group 1-Multivitamin Group 2-Placebo Percent or Mean (Standard Deviation in parenthesis when reported) Percent or Mean (Standard Deviation in parenthesis when reported) Age 70.9 (4.5) 71.0 (4.6) Gender Men 32.9% 33.4% Women 67.1% 66.6% Race/Ethnicity White or European American 93.1% 93.5% Black or African American 2.3% 2.6% Hispanic or Latino/a 1.5% 1.4% Other race or Multiple races 1.8% 1.2% Asian American or Native Hawaiian 0.2% 0.1% American Indian or Alaska Native 1.1% 1.2% Method Once a year for 3 years, participants were asked to complete an online test to evaluate episodic memory and executive functioning. Researchers ensured that the individual participants could not be identified from the data collected. Instructions on how to access the test materials were emailed to participants, and participants who responded to the email indicated that they agreed to be in the study and knew they could withdraw from participation at any time. The participants received a $15 gift card for each annual assessment, regardless of completion. The multivitamin used in this study is widely available in the United States. Researchers considered factors that might impose difficulty for participants. Side effects of taking the multivitamin include low rates of stomach pain, diarrhea, skin rash, bruising, and an increased rate of gastrointestinal bleeding, which are considered normal side effects for those taking a multivitamin in the general population. Two different tasks were used to measure episodic memory and executive functioning in this study: To measure episodic memory, participants completed a recall task in which they were first shown a set of words, presented one at a time for three seconds each. They were then asked to recall the set of words, once immediately after the word list was presented and again after 15 minutes had passed. Participants who recalled more words correctly earned higher scores on this test. To measure executive functioning, participants were first shown one set of items and were then shown a second set of items. They were asked to identify whether items in the second set of items were the same as or different from the first set of items they were shown. Participants earned higher scores the more quickly they correctly identified whether the items from the second set of items were the same as or different from those in the original set. Results and Discussion Compared with participants taking a placebo, participants receiving the multivitamin had significantly greater improvement in the recall task at the end of the first year. Performance on the immediate recall task in Group 1 improved from a mean of 7.10 words at baseline to 7.81 words after 1 year of taking the multivitamin, an improvement mean of 0.71. In Group 2, which received the placebo pills, performance on the immediate recall task improved from a mean of 7.21 words at baseline to 7.65 words after 1 year, an improvement mean of 0.44. When comparing the multivitamin group with the placebo group, averaged across all 3 years of intervention, findings suggest that the memory improvement is sustained over time, as shown in the graph. Comparison of Memory Improvement Between 2 Groups Researchers estimate that the effect of the multivitamin intervention improved memory performance in participants in the multivitamin group by the equivalent of 3.1 years of age-related memory change as compared to participants in the placebo group. Researchers also found that executive functioning was not significantly impacted by taking a multivitamin. When comparing the multivitamin group with the placebo group, averaged across all 3 years of intervention, findings suggest that the memory improvement is sustained over time, as shown in the graph. Comparison of Memory Improvement Between 2 Groups Researchers estimate that the effect of the multivitamin intervention improved memory performance in participants in the multivitamin group by the equivalent of 3.1 years of age-related memory change as compared to participants in the placebo group. Researchers also found that executive functioning was not significantly impacted by taking a multivitamin. The findings suggest that the greatest benefit of taking a multivitamin is found in immediate memory recall, something especially vulnerable in aging adults. Reprinted from The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 118, Yeung, L-K., Alschuler, D. M., Wall, M., Luttman-Gibson, H., Copeland, T., Hale, C., Sloan, R. P., Sesso, H. D., Manson, J. E., Brickman, A. M. Multivitamin supplementation improves memory in older adults: A randomized clinical trial, pages 273–282, Copyright 2023, with permission from Elsevier. Adapted from The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 1155 Your response to the question should be provided in six parts: A, B, C, D, E, and F. Write the response to each part of the question in complete sentences. Use appropriate psychological terminology in your response. F. Explain how at least one of the research findings supports or refutes the researchers’ hypothesis that taking a multivitamin slows cognitive decline in later life.

Propose a specific and defensible claim based in psychological science that responds to the question. Source 1 Introduction Psychologists have been interested in how human performance is affected by interference from stimuli in the environment. Researchers conducted two experiments to determine how much interference the color of the letters of the word affected people’s ability to identify either the word or the color of the letters (e.g., the Stroop task). Participants Experiment 1: 70 college undergraduates participated (14 men and 56 women). Experiment 2: 100 students participated (88 undergraduates; 29 men and 59 women) and 12 graduate students (all women). Experiment 1 tested how quickly participants could read the names of colors. The researcher used two sets of color-name stimuli in this study. The first set of color names (red, blue, green, brown, and purple) was printed in black ink. The second set of color names (also red, blue, green, brown, and purple) was printed in a different color than the name of the color. No word in this set was printed in the color it named, but it was printed an equal number of times in each of the other four colors (e.g., the word “red” was printed in blue, green, brown, and purple inks; the word “blue” was printed in red, green, brown, and purple inks, etc.). Participants were to read the words in each list as fast as they could, ignoring the color of the ink in which the word was printed. Experiment 2 tested how quickly participants could identify the color of the stimulus they were seeing. He used two sets of stimuli for this study. The first set of stimuli printed solid squares in each of the colors used in Experiment 1 (red, blue, green, brown, and purple). In the second set of stimuli, the words were again printed in a color that did not match the name of the color, as described in Experiment 1. Participants were to say the color of the square or of the letters in the word, ignoring the colors named by the words (e.g., where the word “red” was printed in blue ink, the correct response was “blue,” etc.). For both experiments, participants were to read each item in the list without error. If they realized they made an error while reading an item, they were to correct their error as quickly as possible and move forward with the list. A researcher timed each participant using a stopwatch. Results and Discussion Experiment 1: Fourteen participants (20%) left a total of 24 errors uncorrected for the Color Names – Different Ink task, with each participant making no more than 4 errors. Four subjects (5.7%) left one error each uncorrected on the Color Names – Black Ink task. The results are in the table. Mean Time in Seconds for Reading 100 Names of Colors Printed in Colors Different from the Names of the Word and for 100 Names of Colors Printed in Black Gender Number of Participants Color Names – Different Ink Color Names – Black Ink Difference Men 14 43.20 40.81 2.41 Women 56 43.32 41.04 2.28 All participants 70 43.30 41.00 2.30 Experiment 2: Fifty-nine participants (59%) left an average of 2.6 errors uncorrected for the Color of Word – Different Ink task. For the Color of Square task, 32 participants (32%) left an average of 1.2 errors uncorrected. The results are in the table. Mean Time in Seconds for Naming 100 Colors Presented in Squares and 100 Colors Printed in Words which Name Other Colors Gender Number of Participants Color of Word – Different Ink Color of Square Difference Men 29 111.1 69.2 42.9 Women 71 107.5 61.0 46.5 All participants 100 110.3 63.3 47.0 The comparison of the results for Experiment 2 for naming the color of the squares vs naming the color of the letters making up the words (i.e., names of colors) indicates the strength of the interference of the habit of identifying words upon the requested activity of naming the colors of the letters instead. Adapted from Stroop, J. R. (1935). Studies of interference in serial verbal reactions. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 18(6), 643–662. Source 2 Introduction Prior research demonstrated that like other disruptive behaviors, such as arriving late or talking to classmates during lectures, in-class use of personal devices may negatively impact the classroom learning environment. This research examined associations between students’ attitudes toward in-class device use and learning outcomes. Participants Participants were 187 undergraduate students (63.1% women, 36.9% men; mean age = 20.7 years; standard deviation for age = 5.1; range of ages: 17 to 46) at a large urban public university in the northeastern United States. Many students at the college are considered nontraditional based on age and other criteria (e.g., employment, financial independence). In line with the National Center for Education Statistics (2002, 2020), which uses age 24 years and older as an index of nontraditional undergraduate student status, we categorized students in our sample as traditional (ages 17–23 years; number of participants = 164; mode age of 18 years) or nontraditional (ages 24–46 years; number of participants = 23; mode age of 24 years). Method Over the course of the semester, students completed the following online assignments and surveys: Attitudes toward in-class use of personal devices: This six-item survey was given only in Week 2 of the course at the beginning of the semester to assess student attitudes about in-class use of personal mobile devices in relation to learning (“I like to be able to use a personal mobile device during class time”) and whether instructors should permit such usage (“Students should be allowed to use personal mobile devices in the classroom to support their learning”). Participant responded using a 5-point scale (1 = strongly disagree, 3 = neutral, 5 = strongly agree). Concentrating and multitasking while completing schoolwork online: Also in Week 2, students were asked about their ability to concentrate online (“To what extent do you find it hard to concentrate and stay on task when completing course work online?”) and their multitasking behaviors (“To what extent do you multitask [e.g., watch TV or YouTube, check social media, etc.] while completing course work online?”). Participants responded using a 5-point scale (For the questions concerning concentration: “1 = very hard to concentrate, 3 = neither hard nor easy to concentrate, 5 = very easy to concentrate” For the questions concerning frequency of multitasking: “1 = very infrequently, 3 = neutral, 5 = very frequently”). Multiple-choice quizzes: Multiple-choice quizzes were embedded in the online homework assignments administered during Weeks 4, 8, and 12. Each quiz contained 16 to 21 four-option multiple-choice questions on material from class lectures and used as review materials for upcoming tests. Quiz scores (% correct) were averaged across the three homework assignments and used as a measure of learning. End-of-semester technology- and nontechnology-related distractibility: A three-item survey asked students to report the extent to which they were distracted by behaviors related to their own use of personal mobile devices (e.g., laptop, cell phone, tablet, etc.) in course-related contexts (“when completing online assignments, “when reading the textbook,” and “when attending the course”) and by nontechnology-related distractions (“people arriving late,” and “people around them chatting”). Participants responded using a 3-point scale for both contexts (“1 = not distracted, 2 = somewhat distracted, and 3 = very distracted”). Results and Discussion Students held neutral to positive views of in-class use of technology, and mostly agreed that instructors should permit their use. For the question “To what extent do you find it hard to concentrate and stay on task when completing course work online?”, most students held a neutral position but leaned toward saying it was somewhat easy to concentrate while studying online (mean = 3.42; standard deviation = 1.04). In response to the question “To what extent do you multitask (e.g., watch TV or YouTube, check social media, etc.) while completing course work online?”, most students held a neutral view, indicating they multitasked neither frequently nor infrequently (mean = 3.14; standard deviation = 1.22). Researchers failed to find age-related differences in attitudes toward in-class technology use, although they found younger students reported higher rates of multitasking. Age had a negative correlation with students’ self-reported multitasking and a positive correlation with quiz scores. Students’ multitasking tendencies were positively correlated with their attitudes about personal use of mobile devices in class. There was a strong positive correlation between attitudes about the amount of personal device use and how much mobile devices should be permitted in class. Nontraditional students were less likely to report that they multitask online, and they also tended to perform better on the quizzes. However, these two variables (the extent to multitasking while online and quiz performance) were not significantly correlated with each other. Thus, researchers failed to find evidence that multitasking tendencies were on their own predictive of poor learning outcomes or associated with heightened distractibility. There was a statistically significant relationship between better student performance on quizzes and lower self-reported level of distraction using personal mobile devices. Adapted from Ober, T. M., Brodsky, J. E., Yannaco, F.D, and Brooks, P.J. (2023). College instructors’ and students’ views of the use (and misuse) of personal mobile devices. Scholarship of Teaching and Learning in Psychology, 9(1), 14-37. Source 3 Introduction Students often attempt to perform many different tasks at once while attending class and while doing assigned homework. This practice impairs their academic achievement. This problem gets worse when students take online classes where they cannot be directly monitored by a teacher. Prior research has demonstrated that this tendency to do many things at once impairs our ability to recall information because it disrupts the process of getting new information into the memory system. This study investigated whether this negative impact could be reduced by having students use concepts stored in memory to practice what they have learned, instead of just rereading information they have already read in their textbook. Participants The participants were 60 Portuguese-speaking undergraduate students (15 men, 45 women; mean age = 20.75) from Brazil. Method The researchers used three, approximately 700-word-long texts, from a popular scientific journal written in Portuguese. From these seven passages were selected for students to read. While reading, students were interrupted by 3 cell phone messages. Immediately after reading the selected text, students completed a task that differed based on one of three groups to which they were randomly assigned. Condition 1: Students read the selected passages while receiving 3 messages on their cell phone. After doing a 2-minute distraction task, students reread the 7 passages. Condition 2: Students read the selected passages while receiving 3 messages on their cell phone. After doing a 2-minute distraction task, students answered 7 questions (short-answer recall) based on the passages. Condition 3 (control): Students read the selected passages without distraction. After doing a 2-minute distraction task, students answered 7 questions (short-answer recall) based on the passages. At the conclusion of this procedure, students in all groups were asked to report the extent to which they believed they would remember the content of the reading after one week. Researchers called this measurement the “judgment of learning,” or JOL. JOL was measured on a 10-point scale, ranging from 1 (I will remember 0% of the text) to 10 (I will remember 100% of the text). After 7 days, students answered 21 questions drawn from the reading, including the practice items that Groups 2 and 3 had been exposed to previously. Results and Discussion The researchers found, as predicted, that the percentage of correctly recalled answers after one week was lower after multitasking. This indicated a slight negative short-term effect of media multitasking. The researchers found a positive effect 1 week later in the groups that were presented with the practice questions after reading the text. Participants in Condition 1 recalled less information than those in Condition 2 and 3. However, against the researchers’ predictions, the latter two conditions led to equivalent success on the quiz given 7-days later. This suggests that the practice questions minimized the negative effects of media distractions. Retrieving previously encountered information such as taking the quiz in this procedure, is a critical factor for promoting long-term recall of information compared to rereading text passages. This advantageous effect occurred even when to-be-recalled information was entered into the memory system for the first time while distracted by media. In fact, against the researcher’s hypothesis, practice led to equivalent beneficial effects whether information was learned with or without attempting to do multiple things simultaneously. Adapted from Ekuni, R., Macacare, O.T., and Pompeia, S. (2022). Reducing the negative effects of multitasking on online or distance learning by using retrieval practice. Scholarship of Teaching and Learning in Psychology, 8(4). 269-278. 1149 Respond to all parts of the question.

(Source 1)” • Embedded Citation: For example: “According to Source 1, . . .” Write the response to each part of the question in complete sentences. Use appropriate psychological terminology. Question 1: Using the sources provided, develop and justify an argument about the relationship between being in an ordered or cluttered environment and creativity. Propose a specific and defensible claim based in psychological science that responds to the question. (i) Support your claim using at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence from one of the sources. (ii) Explain how the evidence from part B (i) supports your claim using a psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding learned in AP Psychology. (i) Support your claim using an additional piece of specific and relevant evidence from a different source than the one that was used in part B (i). (ii) Explain how the evidence from part C (i) supports your claim using a different psychological perspective, theory, concept, or research finding learned in AP Psychology than the one that was used in part B (ii). Source 1 Introduction Researchers have investigated the effects of being in an orderly/tidy or disorderly/messy environment. In this study, researchers examined whether participants in a messy room would be more creative than participants in an orderly room. Researchers hypothesized that being in a disorderly room encourages people to break with a customary routine and frees them to be more creative. Participants Forty-eight American students participated. Method Participants were randomly assigned to complete a task in an orderly or disorderly room. Picture A shows the orderly condition and picture B shows the disorderly condition. (A) Orderly/Tidy Condition (B) Disorderly/Messy Condition To measure creativity, participants completed an Alternative Uses Task. In this task participants listed up to 10 new uses for ping-pong balls.1 Results and Discussion Participants’ ideas were scored for their creativity. Two raters, unaware of the condition to which participants had been assigned, scored each idea on a 3-point scale (1 = not at all creative, 3 = very creative). If raters disagreed on a score, they resolved it through discussion. Creativity was measured both by averaging creativity scores for each participant and by counting the number of uses participants generated for ping-pong balls. The statistically significant findings are summarized in the table. Condition: Average Creativity Scores: Mean Number of highly creative ideas Messy room 1.80 1.0 Tidy room 1.41 0.21 Researchers believed that the results support their hypothesis that being in a messy room stimulates more creative ideas. In their opinion, being in a messy room disrupts a person’s customary routines resulting in more creative thinking. However, researchers acknowledge another explanation for their findings. It may be that being in a messy room violates participants’ expectations about what a room should look like and that might have increased participant creativity. Adapted from Adapted from Vohs, K. D., Redden, J. P., & Rahinel, R. (2013). Physical Order Produces Healthy Choices, Generosity, and Conventionality, Whereas Disorder Produces Creativity. Psychological Science. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797613480186. Source 2 Introduction In this study, researchers hypothesized that a messy environment influences the cognitive processes that allow individuals to experience critical thinking, thus enabling them to generate, organize, plan, and perform goal-directed behaviors more creatively. Evidence from prior studies suggests that being in a messy environment increases one’s ability to come up with more creative solutions with less reliance on preexisting ways of viewing the world. This study investigated the effect of messy work environments on creativity and these higher-level cognitive processes. Participants One hundred volunteers participated in the study (age: mean 22.89 years, standard deviation = 5.19, 69% women, 31% men, 81% right-handed, 19% left-handed). Methods The study was approved by an Institutional Review Board, and informed consent was obtained prior to participation. Participants were randomly assigned to two adjacent classrooms; one classroom was tidy, and the other was messy. All participants first completed the Alternative Uses Task, in which they generated up to 10 unusual uses for an automobile tire in 5 minutes. Two raters measured the creativity of each participant on a 5-point scale (5 = higher creativity; 1 = lower creativity) by considering how commonplace, unusual, and clever the participant’s responses were. When the ratings of the independent raters were compared on their creativity scores, they were highly correlated. To measure cognitive processing, participants completed additional tests. In one test, called a digit span task, participants were given a series of numbers and asked to repeat back what they had heard. In each subsequent trial a longer sequence of numbers was presented. The longest remembered sequence was the measure of the participant’s digit span. Researchers also included a letter fluency test, in which participants were given 1 minute to produce as many unique words as possible starting with a given letter, either “L” or “F.” Participants were debriefed and compensated with either a $20.00 gift card or extra credit for their introductory psychology course at the completion of the study. Results and Discussion Researchers compared the creativity and cognitive processing measures between the tidy and messy conditions. Results indicated that working at a messy desk did not result in significant and reliable differences in creativity or in the performance of tasks related to higher level cognitive processing. The results from the described tests are summarized in the table. Condition: Alternative Uses Task Creativity Mean Digit Span Mean Letter Fluency Mean Correct Messy room 2.68 4.02 22.98 Tidy room 2.80 4.44 21.96 Researchers identified some possible limitations to their study that might explain the lack of statistically significant differences in the results obtained from the two conditions. One earlier study asked participants to develop alternative uses for a ping-pong ball using a 3-point creativity scale, while this study used an automobile tire, and a 5-point scale. However, this research used many different measures of creativity and of higher-level cognitive processing, and none showed the expected effect. Adapted from Manzi, A., Durmysheva, Y., Pinegar, S. K., Rogers, A., & Ramos, J. (2019). Workspace Disorder Does Not Influence Creativity and Executive Functions. Frontiers in Psychology, 9(2662), 1-6. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2018.02662. Source 3 Introduction An orderly environment can affect both an individual’s ability to control their behaviors and the amount of their creative thinking; however, little research has focused on the role personality factors may have in strengthening or weakening this effect. In this study, researchers investigated the effect of self-control as a stable personality trait which they believe influences both self-control behaviors and creative thinking. Participants Experiment 1: One hundred and sixty-three participants (127 women, 36 men, mean age 19.80 years, standard deviation = 1.7) volunteered to participate. Experiment 2: One hundred and twenty-four participants (101 women, 23 men, mean age 20.38, standard deviation = 1.28) volunteered to participate in a follow-up experiment after being a part of Experiment 1. All participants were students at a university in China. Method The experiments were approved by the Institutional Review Board of the School of Psychology. Each participant signed an informed written consent form. Experiment 1: Participants completed a self-report survey to measure their typical level of self-control. Questions included statements such as, “I do certain things that are bad for me if they are fun,” and “I have self-discipline.” Each statement was evaluated from 1 “Not at all like me” to 5 “Very much like me.” Based on this survey, they were sorted into two levels of self-control, either “high trait self-control” or “low trait self-control.” All participants were then asked to hold their breath “for as long as you are able or until you have to give up,” and the experimenter timed the duration with a stopwatch. After establishing each participant’s breath-holding ability in a neutral space, participants were randomly assigned to either an orderly or a disorderly room. As pictured, in the orderly condition (A), books, pens, and other items were arranged in a structured and ordered way on a table. In the disorderly condition (B), the same quantity of items was scattered on the table or on the floor. Participants were asked to wait for one minute while the experimenter got materials ready, and then they were once again asked to hold their breath and data was collected. (A) Orderly/Tidy Condition (B) Disorderly/Messy Condition Experiment 2: The same participants from Experiment 1 were invited to take part in a follow-up experiment. They were randomly assigned to different conditions. As pictured, in the orderly condition (A), participants were shown a picture of a desk with well-organized items. In the disorderly condition (B), participants were shown a picture of a desk with the same items scattered on the table. (A) Orderly/Tidy Condition (B) Disorderly/Messy Condition The participants imagined that they were in the scene. After 3 minutes, participants completed an Alternative Uses Task where they were asked to list as many “uncommon, creative, and unusual” uses of tires as possible within 3 minutes. Participants’ ideas were scored by five research assistants who were unaware of the different conditions to which each participant was assigned. Each response received a rating on a five-point scale (1 = low creativity, 5 = high creativity). Responses that scored more than 3 points, were labeled as “highly creative ideas.” Results and Discussion Experiment 1: Researchers found that participants who scored low in trait self-control held their breath longer in the orderly room than in the disorderly room. There was no difference in breath-holding ability for participants who scored high in trait self-control when they were in either the orderly or disorderly room. These findings are represented in the graph. Experiment 2: For the participants with high trait self-control, those in the disorderly condition generated more creative ideas (mean = 1.62) than those in the orderly condition (mean = 1.00). This difference was statistically significant. Researchers concluded that an orderly environment could have a positive influence on individuals’ self-control behaviors. However, this applied only to those with low trait self-control. In contrast, a disorderly environment can be beneficial to creative thinking, but this applied mostly for individuals with high trait self-control. The findings are represented in the graph. Adapted from Li, Z., & Li, S. (2020). Environmental Orderliness Affects Self-Control and Creative Thinking: The Moderating Effects of Trait Self-Control. Frontiers in Psychology, 11, 1-13. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2020.01515. 2032 Respond to all parts of the question.
